title: Basvaraj S/O Kori Channabasappa Since ... vs Jayasheela W/O Jayalingappa on 21 November, 2022
body: Basvaraj S/O Kori Channabasappa Since ... vs Jayasheela W/O Jayalingappa on 21 November, 2022 1 IN THE HIGH COURT OF KARNATAKA AT BENGALURU DATED THIS THE 21ST DAY OF NOVEMBER, 2022 BEFORE THE HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE ANANT RAMANATH HEGDE REGULAR SECOND APPEAL NO.2708 OF 2006 C/W REGULAR SECOND APPEAL NO.2707 OF 2006 IN RSA NO.2708/2006: BETWEEN: BASAVARAJ, S/O KORI CHANNABASAPPA, SINCE DECEASED BY LRS. 1. KORI VEERANNA, S/O BASAVARAJ, AGED ABOUT 44 YEARS. 2. K. GIRIRAJ, S/O. BASAVARAJ, AGED ABOUT 38 YEARS. 3. JAYAPPA, DEAD BY HIS LRs. 3.(a) SMT.KORI SHIVAMMA, W/O LATE JAYAPPA, AGED ABOUT 63 YEARS. 3.(b) RAJASHEKHARA, S/O LATE JAYAPPA, AGED ABOUT 40 YEARS. 3.(c) SMT.SHANTHA, D/O LATE JAYAPPA, AGED ABOUT 38 YEARS. 2 3.(d) SMT.HEMAVATHI, D/O LATE JAYAPPA, AGED ABOUT 36 YEARS, 3.(e) SMT.MADHUMATHI, D/O LATE JAYAPPA, AGED ABOUT 34 YEARS. 3.(f) PRAVEEN, S/O. LATE JAYAPPA, AGED ABOUT 32 YEARS, 3.(g) MAHARUDRA, S/O. LATE JAYAPPA, AGED ABOUT 30 YEARS, ALL ARE R/AT NAGAPPANA KERI, 2ND CROSS, GANDHIBAZAR, SHIVAMOGGA - 577 201. 4. NAGARAJ, S/O. KORI CHANNAPPA, AGED ABOUT 55 YEARS ALL ARE RESIDING AT NAGAPPAN KERI 2ND CROSS, GANDHI BAZAAR, SHIVAMOGGA. ... APPELLANTS (BY SRI A.V.GANGADARAPPA A/W SRI K.B.LOKANATH, ADVOCATE) AND: 1. JAYASHEELA, W/O JAYALINGAPPA, AGED ABOUT 50 YEARS. 2. JAYALINGAPPA, SINCE DEAD BY HIS LR'S 2.(a) VEERESH, S/O LATE JAYALINGAPPA, AGED ABOUT 36 YEARS. 3 2.(b) SMT.KAVITHA D.J, D/O LATE JAYALINGAPPA, AGED ABOUT 33 YEARS. 2.(c) KUM. MAMATHA, D/O LATE JAYALINGAPPA, AGED ABOUT 28 YEARS, ALL ARE RESIDING AT NAGAPPAN KERI, 2ND CROSS, GANDHI BAZAAR, SHIVAMOGGA. ... RESPONDENTS (BY SMT.PRAMILA NESARGI, SR.COUNSEL A/W SMT.GEETHA MENON, ADVOCATE FOR C/R1 & R2(A-C)) THIS RSA IS FILED U/S. 100 OF CPC AGAINST THE JUDGEMENT & DECREE DATED 31.7.2006 PASSED IN R.A.NO.46/1989 ON THE FILE OF THE PRESIDING OFFICER, FAST TRACK COURT-I, SHIMOGA, DISMISSING THE APPEAL AND CONFIRMING THE JUDGEMENT AND DECREE DATED 08.02.1982 PASSED IN OS.NO.34/1977 ON THE FILE OF THE ADDL. CIVIL JUDGE & C.J.M., SHIMOGA. IN RSA NO.2707/2006: BETWEEN: BASAVARAJ, S/O KORI CHANNABASAPPA, SINCE DECEASED BY LRS. 1. KORI VEERANNA, S/O BASAVARAJ, AGED ABOUT 44 YEARS. 2. K. GIRIRAJ, S/O BASAVARAJ, AGED ABOUT 38 YEARS. 3. JAYAPPA, AGED ABOUT 58 YEARS SINCE DEAD BY HIS LRS 4 3.(a) SMT.KORI SHIVAMMA W/O. LATE JAYAPPA AGED ABOUT 63 YEARS. 3.(b) RAJASHEKHARA, S/O LATE JAYAPPA, AGED ABOUT 40 YEARS. 3.(c) SMT.SHANTHA, D/O LATE JAYAPPA, AGED ABOUT 38 YEARS. 3.(d) SMT.HEMAVATHI, D/O LATE JAYAPPA, AGED ABOUT 36 YEARS, 3.(e) SMT.MADHUMATHI, D/O LATE JAYAPPA, AGED ABOUT 34 YEARS. 3.(f) PRAVEEN, S/O LATE JAYAPPA, AGED ABOUT 32 YEARS, 3.(g) MAHARUDRA, S/O LATE JAYAPPA, AGED ABOUT 30 YEARS, ALL ARE R/AT NAGAPPANA KERI, 2ND CROSS, GANDHIBAZAR, SHIVAMOGGA - 577 201. 4. NAGARAJ, S/O KORI CHANNAPPA, AGED ABOUT 55 YEARS ALL ARE R/AT NAGAPPANA KERI 2ND CROSS, GANDHI BAZAAR, SHIVAMOGGA. ... APPELLANTS (BY SRI A.V.GANGADARAPPA A/W SRI K.B.LOKANATH, ADVOCATES) 5 AND: 1. JAYASHEELA, W/O. JAYALINGAPPA AGED ABOUT 50 YEARS. 2. JAYALINGAPPA SINCE DEAD BY HIS LR'S 2.(a) VEERESH, S/O LATE JAYALINGAPPA, AGED ABOUT 36 YEARS. 2.(b) SMT.KAVITHA D.J., D/O LATE JAYALINGAPPA, AGED ABOUT 33 YEARS. 2.(c) KUM. MAMATHA, D/O LATE JAYALINGAPPA, AGED ABOUT 28 YEARS, ALL ARE RESIDING AT NAGAPPAN KERI, 2ND CROSS, GANDHI BAZAAR, SHIVAMOGGA. ... RESPONDENTS (BY SMT.PRAMILA NESARGI, SR.COUNSEL A/W SMT.GEETHA MENON, ADVOCATE FOR C/R1 & R2(A-C)) THIS RSA IS FILED UNDER SECTION 100 OF THE CODE OF CIVIL PROCEDURE AGAINST THE JUDGMENT AND DECREE DATED 31.07.2006 PASSED IN R.A.NO.45/1989 ON THE FILE OF THE PRESIDING OFFICER, FAST TRACK COURT-I, SHIMOGA, DISMISSING THE APPEAL AND CONFIRMING THE JUDGMENT AND DECREE DATED: 08.02.1982 PASSED IN OS NO.17/1977 ON THE FILE OF THE ADDL. CIVIL JUDGE AND C.J.M., SHIMOGA. THESE APPEALS COMING ON FOR FURTHER DICTATION THIS DAY, THE COURT DELIVERED THE FOLLOWING: 6 JUDGMENT Regular Second Appeals No.2708/2006 and 2707/2006 are arising from a common judgment and decrees passed in O.S.No.17/1977 and O.S. no.34/1977 on the file of Civil Judge, Shimoga. Both the suits are clubbed together and common evidence was recorded and disposed of by the common judgment in terms of judgment and decrees dated. 05.11.1977. 2. O.S. No.17/1977 was filed for partition and separate possession of 3/4th share in the suit schedule properties on the premise that the suit schedule properties were joint family properties of late Kori Veerappa and Kori Channappa who were brothers. The suit was filed by the children of Kori Channappa against the legatee of Kori Shantamma, who is the wife of Kori Veerappa. Said suit is dismissed. The trial court held that both the suit properties self-acquired properties of Kori Veerappa and on his demise same is inherited by his wife Kori Shantamma and later inherited by legatees of Kori Shantamma in terms of the registered Will dated 08.05.1974 executed by Kori Shantamma. 7 3. O.S.No.34/1977 is a suit for declaration of the title in respect of suit A and B schedule properties based on a registered Will dated 08.05.1974 referred above and also the suit for possession of 'B' schedule property. B schedule property is a residential house. The said suit was decreed in favour of the plaintiffs in O.S.no.34/1977. 4. R.A. No.45/1989 is filed before Senior Civil Judge, Shimoga, impugning the judgment and decree in O.S. no. 17/1977. And R.A. no.46/1989 is filed impugning the judgment and decree passed in O.S.No.34/1977. The appeals are dismissed. Hence these two-second appeals. 5. The plaintiffs in O.S.No.17/1977 who are the defendants in O.S. No.34/1977, have filed RSA No.2708/2006 and RSA No.2707/2006 respectively. 6. The suit for partition is filed in respect of two properties. Item no.1 property is the residential house in Shimoga. Item no.2 is a land situated in Mudlakere village, Shimoga taluk which measures 1 acre 3 guntas. 8 7. The genealogy of the parties is as under: GENEALOGYOGY Kori Basappa KORI VEERAPPA KORI CHANNAPPA SHANTHAMMA (WIFE) RUDRAMMA (WIFE) BASAVARAJAPPA JAYAPPA NAGARAJ (PLAINTIFF-1) (PLAINTIFF-2) (PLAINTIFF-3) BY 1ST WIFE JAYASHEELA BY 2ND WIFE (1ST DEFENDANT) VEEERANNA WIFE OF JAYALINGAPPA GIRIYAPPA (2ND DEFENDANT) 8. One Kori Basappa was the propositus. He had two sons, Kori Veerappa and Kori Channappa. Kori Veerappa had a wife by the name of Shanthamma. Kori Veerappa died in the year 1941. Kori Channappa- the brother of Kori Veerappa died in the year 1951. Shanthamma, the wife of Kori Veerappa died in the year 1976. Admittedly, Kori Veerappa and Shanthamma had no issues from their marriage. Kori Channappa had 3 sons, namely Basavarajappa, Jayappa, and Nagaraj. 9 9. Kori Shanthamma had a sister. Said sister had a daughter, Akkamahadevi. Said Akkamahadevi was married to Basavarajappa, the elder son of Kori Channappa. From the marriage, Basavarajappa and Akkamahadevi had a daughter by the name of Jayasheela. Admittedly, within 11 days of the birth of Jayasheela, her mother Akkamahadevi died. Thereafter, Basavarajappa married for the second time. 10. Suit in O.S. no.17/1977 (hereinafter referred to as appellants) is filed on the premise that Kori Veerappa and Kori Channappa formed a joint family and jointly possessed the suit properties. It is alleged that after the demise of Kori Veerappa, his wife Kori Shanthamma was looking after the family affairs. Kori Channappa died in the year 1951 and appellants were residing along with Kori Shanthamma in the joint family house. After the demise of Kori Shanthamma, respondents started claiming exclusive rights over the properties based on the Will of Kori Shanthamma. Hence, the suit was filed claiming partition. 11. The Respondents claim that the suit properties exclusively belonged to Kori Veerappa and after the 10 demise of Kori Veerappa, his wife Kori Shanthamma acquired absolute title over the properties. It is also the case of the respondents that Kori Shantamma executed a Will in their favour and accordingly prayed for the dismissal of the suit. 12. The Respondents filed O.S.No.34/1977 filed the suit against the children of Channappa for declaration of title over the suit properties based on the Will dated 08.05.1974 executed by Kori Shanthamma and sought for possession of the 'B' schedule property on the premise that the appellants are in permissive possession of the property. 13. The Trial Court has concluded that the properties are self-acquired properties of Shanthamma and also Will executed by Shanthamma established and consequently dismissed the suit in O.S.No.17/1977 and has decreed O.S.No.34/1977. 14. The First Appellate Court dismissed the appeals filed by the appellants. 11 15. The appeals were admitted vide order dated 11.01.2011 to consider the following substantial question of law. "Whether the Courts below were justified in holding that the Will Ex.D-1 has been proved in the absence of acceptable material on record and whether the appreciation of evidence is capricious and illegal?" 16. Later vide order dated 30.04.2015 one more substantial question of law was framed as under: "Whether both the Courts have committed serious illegality by ignoring and misinterpreting the material evidence on record while concluding that Kori Channappa and Kori Veerappa did not live as members of joint family as on the date of Kori Veerappa and as such Shanthamma was not competent to execute the Will?''. 17. This Court has noticed that the substantial question of law framed above contains a typographical error. It is to be noted that Kori Shantamma was held to be competent to execute the Will by both courts. Thus the 12 word 'not' appearing in a substantial question of law is an error and said the question is reframed as under. "Whether both the Courts have committed serious illegality by ignoring and misinterpreting the material evidence on record while concluding that Kori Channappa and Kori Veerappa did not live as members of joint family as on the date of Kori Veerappa and as such Shanthamma was competent to execute the Will"? 18. Sri.A.V.Gangagadharappa, appearing for the appellants would raise the following contentions: (i) The documents at Ex.P-1 to P11 would demonstrate that Kori Veerappa and Kori Channappa lived together under one roof carrying business together as joint family members and all the properties standing in the name of Kori Veerappa should be construed as the joint family properties of Kori Veerappa and his younger brother Kori Channappa. (ii) The Ex.D3-the inventory report filed by the Court Commissioner who was appointed in the suit filed by the Kori Channappa seeking partition against his 13 brother in the year 1935-36. The inventory prepared by the court commissioner reveals that Kori Channappa and Kori Veerappa lived together under one roof. (iii) The fact that the property belonging to the joint family was sold together by Kori Channappa and Kori Veerappa and one of the properties was given on rent by Kori Channappa and Kori Veerappa and the fact that they jointly borrowed hand loans by executing promissory notes would also demonstrate that the joint family business and joint family was very much in existence. (iv) The execution of the alleged Will by Kori Shanthamma is shrouded with suspicious circumstances, and the burden on the propounder is not discharged to prove its due execution. (v) The evidence of the attesting witnesses and the scribe would indicate that Kori Shanthamma did not have the intention to execute the Will and she was forced to execute the Will under some compulsion. 14 (vi) The attesting witnesses examined on behalf of the beneficiary under the Will have not proved the attestation and execution of the Will. (vii) Alternatively, it is contended that Kori Shanthamma did not have absolute rights over the properties. At the most, she could have executed a Will for only over ½ share in the suit schedule properties. 19. Learned Senior counsel Smt. Prameela Nesargi, appearing for the respondents, supporting the impugned judgment and decrees would raise the following contentions: (i) The property at serial no.1 was purchased in the year 1916 (Ex-P1) in the name of Kori Veerappa and Mallappa, who is not a family member and one year later, said Mallapa sold his right in favor of Kori Veerappa (Ex-P2) and such, it is Veerappa's self- acquired property and Kori Channappa was not a party to the transaction under Ex.P1 and P2. (ii) The family was in a great financial crisis; accordingly, the family property i.e., the residential 15 house belonging to the brothers was sold by Kori Veerappa and Kori Channappa in 1925. Since they were in great financial difficulty they occupied the same property as tenants. This factor would establish that there was no sufficient income for the family. (iii) The promissory notes relied upon by the plaintiffs would only indicate that the brothers borrowed the money and carried the business together and nothing beyond. The suit schedule properties were purchased between 1916-1918 and the promissory notes executed were of the year 1928-30, as such the income said to have been generated from the joint family business carried on from the borrowings made in 1928-30, cannot be said to have been utilized for acquiring the properties between 1916- 18. (iv) The suit for partition was filed by Kori Channappa in the year 1935-36 and the same was dismissed for non-payment of Court fees. Kori Channappa did not prosecute the suit further. 16 (v) Kori Channappa died in the year 1951. Though he lived for 15 years after the dismissal of the suit, he did not claim partition. Kori Veerappa died in the year 1941, even then there was no action taken by Kori Channappa to seek partition. (vi) Out of 3 acres of land in Sy. No.17 which was purchased in the year 1918, Kori Shanthamma sold two acres in Mudlukere village, Shimoga taluk, after the demise of Kori Veerappa, and the children of Kori Channappa did not object to the sale transaction. The suit is filed for the remaining 1 acre and the same is not maintainable. (vii) The rental agreements are executed by Kori Shantamma where she transferred the property to the tenants in her individual capacity and these documents would establish that Kori Channappa is not a joint family member and the properties were exclusive properties of Kori Shanthamma as she inherited the same through her husband. (viii) In the cause title of the suit filed by Kori Channappa, his address is shown as a resident of Bikkenahalli, 17 Taluk Shimoga and his act of filing the suit would demonstrate that Kori Channappa never stayed as a joint family member of Kori Veerappa. (ix) Kori Shanthamma's sister's daughter Akkamahadevi married Basavarajappa- the son of Kori Channappa. Akkamahadevi died 11 days after the birth of her daughter Jayasheela and thereafter Kori Shanthamma looked after Jayasheela. Basavarajapa married for the second time and thereafter he did not properly look after his daughter as such the relationship was strained and Kori Shantamma had every reason to execute the Will in favour of Jayasheela and she executed and registered the same and execution is duly proved. (x) There is no pleading to the effect that the joint family comprising Kori Veerappa and Kori Channappa did possess sufficient joint family nucleus to acquire the property. (xi) There is no pleading disputing the Will of Kori Shanthamma. The only contention raised in the plaint is that she could not have executed the Will in 18 excess of ½ share and there is no other challenge to the Will. No declaration is sought challenging the Will. Discussion regarding proof of the Will dated 08.05.1974 20. In paragraph No.7 of the plaint in O.S. no.17/1977 it is pleaded that Kori Shantamma died in the month of November 1976, and 5 to 6 years before her death, her relationship with the plaintiffs was not cordial. It is further stated that when the partition was demanded and a meeting was held in the presence of the well-wishers in the said meeting, the Will alleged to have been executed by Kori Shantamma was disclosed. However, no contention is raised in the plaint denying the execution of the alleged Will by Shantamma. In his cross-examination, Pw1 states that he came to know about the Will of Kori Shantamma two years before the death of Kori Shantamma. What is essentially pleaded in the plaint is that Shantamma could not have executed a Will on the premise the properties are not her exclusive properties and at the most, she could have executed the Will only in respect of her share. There 19 is no plea of fraud and misrepresentation, coercion, or undue influence. This Court has also considered the prayer made in the plaint. There is no prayer to declare the Will invalid. The signature of Kori Shantamma on the Will is not disputed. If the plaint in O. S.17 / 77 is read along with a written statement filed in O. S.34 / 77 , it is apparent that the children of Chakkanna hardly disputed the signature, execution, and sound disposing state of mind of Kori Shantamma. Thus the evidence relating to the Will and finding on the Will by the trial court and appellate court will have to be considered keeping in mind the contentions in the pleading noted above. 21. From the evidence of the attesting witness and the evidence of the scribe, on certain facts viz., who were all present when the Will was executed, where the Will was drafted, who were all present when the Will was registered, it can be noticed that there are certain discrepancies. By referring to these discrepancies and also based on the fact that the Will was not registered on the date when it was drafted and same was registered a few days later it is urged that Kori Shantamma was under 20 pressure to execute the Will at the instance of Shekarappa the 2nd defendant. 22. Referring to the evidence of DW2 where he has stated that he has attested only one document, when admittedly, two documents are registered on that day i.e., one purporting to be the Will of Kori Shantamma and another being cancellation of the earlier Will alleged to have been executed by Kori Shantamma, it is contended that whether witness attested the Will or document canceling the earlier Will is not forthcoming. Thus it is urged that there is no positive evidence to suggest that attesting witness did attest to the Will. Thus it is contended that the finding of the trial court and the appellate court on proof of Will is based on a gross misinterpretation of material evidence. 23. Learned Senior counsel Smt. Pramila Nesargi in support of her contention would submit that the evidence is recorded many years after the execution of the Will and there cannot be a mathematical precision when it comes to the evidence of an attesting witness and the 21 scribe relating to the aspects highlighted by the appellants. If at all there are any discrepancies in the evidence of the attesting witnesses and the scribe, those discrepancies cannot be construed as discrepancies to disbelieve the Will. It is urged that once the trial Court and the First Appellate Court have concluded that the execution of the Will is duly established, the Court in the exercise of power under Section 100 of the Code of Civil Procedure is not authorized to re-examine the evidence and appreciate the evidence on record unless the appreciation of evidence by the trial Court and the First Appellate Court is shown to be capricious and contrary to the established principles of law. 24. It is not in dispute that Kori Shantamma did not have any children from her marriage. Her husband Kori Veerappa died in 1941. Kori Shantamma had a sister and her sister's daughter Akkamahadevi was married to Basavaraj the 1st defendant, who is the son of Kori Channappa. From this marriage between Akkamahadevi and Basavaraj, a daughter by the name of Jayasheela was born. On the 11th day after the birth of Jayasheela, Akkamahadevi, the wife of Basavaraj died. It is 22 forthcoming from the records that Jayasheela was brought up by Kori Shantamma and Kori Shantamma performed the marriage of Jayasheela with Jayalingappa. In the absence of any Will by Kori Shantammma, the properties would devolve on the heirs of Kori Chanappa and Jayasheela would be left with no property. This is one of the circumstances which would strongly suggest that Kori Shantamma had every reason to execute a Will in favour of Jayasheela who was raised by Kori Shantamma after the demise of Jayasheela's mother who died on the 11th day after the birth of Jayasheela. 25. From the circumstances noticed above and also considering the fact that there is no specific denial or dispute relating to the signature of Kori Shantamma on the disputed registered Will at Ex.D.1, and there is no allegation and evidence to suggest that Korsi Shantamma was not possessing sound state of disposing mind, this Court is of the view that minor discrepancies appearing in the evidence of attesting witness as well as the scribe would not come in the way of Court holding that the Will is duly executed. 23 26. What is required to be noted is the trial Court as well as the First Appellate court have concurrently given a finding that Will executed by Kori Shantamma is duly proved. Since this Court is exercising the jurisdiction under Section 100 Code of Civil Procedure, the question that needs to be answered is, merely because an alternative view could have been envisaged, same cannot be a ground to interfere in the concurrent finding under Section 100 Code of Civil Procedure unless the appreciation of evidence is shown to be contrary to the established principle of law governing the interpretation of evidence. 27. The evidence of the attesting witness that he has attested only one document on that day, though his signatures are found on two documents, namely the Will and cancelation of the earlier Will, is not sufficient to hold that he has not attested the Will. At the most, it is possible to argue that either he has attested the Will or deed canceling the earlier Will and not both. Fact remains that he has signed both documents and both documents are registered. The statement that he has signed only one 24 document cannot be construed to say that he has not executed the Will. However, on appreciation of circumstances, and other evidence on record and given the fact that the Will is registered and Kori Shantamma survived almost two years after the execution of the Will and also in view of the fact that there is hardly any challenge to the execution of the Will in the pleading, the concurrent finding of the courts relating to due execution Will in favour of defendants in O.S. no.17/1977 and plaintiffs in O.S. NO.34/1977 has to be confirmed. Discussion regarding the nature of item no.2 Property. 28. The contention that the suit schedule properties are the joint family properties of Kori Veerappa and Kori Channappa is tried to be substantiated with reference to the existence of the joint family business. It is urged that the brothers together borrowed a loan and brothers sold the joint family house. There is evidence relating to the sale of the joint family house on 22.04.1925. The sale deed is produced at Ex.P.5. The plaintiffs have also produced the promissory notes for 25 having borrowed the loan. These documents are of the year 1928 to 1930. There is no difficulty in holding that the brother carried the business together. However, item No. 2 property is purchased on 20.04.1918 in the name of Kori Veerappa. 29. The original title deeds pertaining to both schedule properties were produced by the appellants. It is urged that the production of original title deeds by the appellants would establish their claim relating to joint family properties. 30. Learned Senior counsel appearing for the respondents refuting the contention would submit that children of Kori Chanappa, after the marriage of Basavaraj lived with Kori Shantamma in item No.1 house and then without the knowledge of Kori Shantamma have taken the title deeds of item Nos.1 and 2 properties and mere production of original title deed would not lead to the conclusion that the properties are joint family properties. 31. The claim relating to joint ownership of item no.2 property cannot be accepted based on the production 26 of the original title deed pertaining to item no.2 property in the backdrop of certain other evidence on record. Survey no. 12 was originally agricultural land. In the cross- examination, PW-1 admits that much before the filing of the suit, Kori Shantamma sold the property bearing Sy. No.12 of Mullakeri Village, Taluk Shimoga. Appellants did not object. Pw1 admitted that the plaintiffs have not claimed any right over the property measuring 2 acres sold by Kori Shantamma in Sy. No.12. It is further stated that Shantamma received around Rs.80,000=00 in the 1970s from the sale of part of Sy. No.12. The suit is filed in respect of the remaining unsold portion in Sy. No.12. Though a major portion in Sy. no.12 is sold no objections are raised which is an important circumstance that would strongly suggest that the property was never treated as joint family property. Under the circumstances, the findings of the trial Court and the First Appellate Court relating to item No.2 as the self-acquired property of Kori Veerappa do not call for any interference. 32. It is also to be noticed that apart from a lack of evidence relating to joint acquisition there is no other 27 circumstantial evidence to hold that the item No.2 property is a joint family property. Thus this court is of the view that are no materials to hold that the concurrent findings of the Trial Court as well as the First Appellate Court are erroneous as far as item no.2 property is concerned. Discussion regarding the nature of item No.1 property 33. As far as item No.1 property which is a residential house is concerned, a different approach needs to be adopted to analyse the evidence for the simple reason that the appellants are admittedly occupying the portion of the property. 34. The respondents have sought possession of the 'B' schedule property on the premise that the appellants are in permissive possession. Thus, the Court has to consider whether the plea of permissive possession is established. If the plea relating to permissive possession is not established, and if the appellants can establish they were in possession much earlier in the period than asserted by the respondents, and if there are other vital evidence or circumstances to suggest that the property is 28 the joint property, then the case of the appellants would become more probable than that of the respondents. 35. The plaint in O.S.No.34/1977 would reveal that after the marriage of Akkamahadevi, Kori Shanthamma during her lifetime, had permitted the 1st defendant (appellant No.1) to live in the portion of the property temporarily. It is stated that Kori Shanthamma demanded the 1st defendant to vacate the 'B' schedule property which belonged to her. Instead of vacating the same, the 1st defendant brought his two brothers i.e., defendants No.2 and 3 (appellants No.2 and 3) and they started living there. After the death of Kori Shanthamma, plaintiffs No.1 and 2 demanded defendants No.1 and 2 hand over the 'B' schedule property which belonged to them. 36. The contention found in paragraph 7 of the plaint, in O.S.No.34/1977, if considered with reference to the written statement in O.S.No.17/1977, particularly in paragraph 8 of the written statement, wherein, it is stated that the appellants are in possession of the portion of the property, the contradiction in the statement of the respondents relating to permissive possession is apparent. 29 In O.S.No.34/1977, it is stated by the plaintiffs that only the 1st defendant (appellant No.1) was permitted to occupy the property and despite Kori Shanthamma asking the 1 st defendant to vacate the property, defendants No. 2 and 3 started living in it. In other words, it is as good as saying that appellants no.1 and 2 are not in permissive possession and they are trespassers. Whereas, in the written statement in paragraph 8, it is contended that Kori Shanthamma permitted all the defendants to occupy the property. This creates doubt about the stand of the respondents relating to the permissive possession of the appellants over the suit property. The contradiction in the pleading has not been noticed by Trial Court as well as the First Appellate Court. 37. It is borne out from records that the original title deeds dated 24.06.1916 and 05.12.2017 pertaining to item no.1 property are also produced at Ex.P-1 and Ex.P-2 by the plaintiffs in O. S.17 / 77 . And the possession of part of the item no.1 property is with the appellants. In addition to this the inventory report submitted in the suit for partition filed by Kori Chanappa, in the year 1936, it is 30 stated that one of the safe lockers was found in item No.1 property and the lock pertaining to that particular safe locker was with Kori Chanappa and Kori Chanappa opened the lock in the presence of the Commissioner and this fact would indicate that Kori Chanappa had control over the safe locker found in the suit house i.e., item No.1 property and same was jointly possessed by Kori Chanappa and Kori Veerappa. 38. The respondents contend that the suit was filed in the year 1935-36 seeking partition by Kori Chanappa against Kori Veerappa. The suit was dismissed for non-payment of the court fee. Thereafter, the matter was not pursued further and this would indicate that Kori Veerappa had given up his claim that the properties are joint family properties. Kori Chanappa died in 1951. For 26 years, there was no action by the children of Kori Chanappa seeking partition. It is also the case of the respondents that the address of the parties shown in the cause title of the suit filed in 1935 would indicate that Kori Chanappa and Kori Veerappa lived in different places and Kori Chanappa never lived in Shivamogga, as such he is 31 not a member of the joint family and the brothers would not constitute a joint family. 39. It is also urged that after the death of Kori Veerappa who died in 1941 and who was admittedly carrying on the Opium/ganja business, an application is filed by Kori Channappa for the release of a security deposit with the Government. Kori Chanappa who claimed to be the heir of Kori Veerappa to succeed in the amount in deposit. The application was rejected by the subordinate Court in Shivamogga. 40. This Court has perused the cause title of the parties shown in the 1935 proceeding. Kori Channapa the plaintiff in the said case has claimed to be a resident of Shivamogga Town as well as Bikkenahalli. This is evident from Ex.D-6 which is the copy of the application filed by Kori Chanappa when he filed a case claiming right over the security deposit made by Kori Chanappa to carry on his business in Opium. Ex.D.5 is the document relating to Misc. Case No.222/35-36 on the file of the Civil Judge, Shivamogga where Kori Chanappa filed a case for partition and he has shown his address as a resident of Doddapet, 32 Shivamogga where item No.1 property is located and Kori Veerappa is also shown to be in the same address. The suit is filed in the capacity of an indigent person. 41. The pleading relating to joint family acquisition does not give the details as to why the property was acquired only in the name of Kori Veerappa, and not in the joint name of Veerappa and Channappa. The claim relating to the joint family property has to be considered and analysed in the backdrop of the fact that when the property was purchased in 1916 and 1917, the appellants were not even born. The suit was filed in 1977. Not furnishing details of the transactions which occurred many decades before their birth cannot be a ground to reject the claim for partition when the parties have contested the matter understanding the basis of the claim. Thus want of particulars in the pleading relating to the joint family nucleus is not of much consequence. 42. Exhibit P-4 is the lease agreement executed by Kori Veerappa and Kori Channappa. This document reveals that Kori Veerappa and Kori Channappa have vacated the 33 property which they have sold under Ex.P5-registered sale deed in respect of the property which they admitted is joint family property. On reading Ex.P4, it can be safely concluded that Kori Veerappa and Kori Channappa jointly lived in a particular house. After vacating the said house, in the absence of any other evidence, it can be concluded that they lived in the suit house i.e., item no.1 property as there are materials to show that they were carrying on business together as could be seen from joint promissory notes executed in the years 1928, 1929, and 1930. 43. Ex.P-11 is an important document that would reveal the joint ownership of Kori Veerappa and Kori Channappa. It is an agreement executed by a tenant in favour of Kori Veerappa and Kori Channappa for having occupied a portion of item No.1 property on a monthly lease. Though this document is not signed by either Kori Veerappa or Kori Channappa, there is no cross- examination on this document suggesting that this document is not pertaining to item No.1 property or that the brothers did not let the property on rent. Both the Courts have not considered this document. Apart from that 34 Ex.P-6 is also claimed to be an agreement evidencing lease in respect of the portion of item No.1 property. Even this document does not bear the signature of Kori Veerappa and Kori Channappa. The tenant who claims to have occupied a portion of item No.1 property has executed this document on 03.06.1928 recognising ownership of Kori Veerappa and Kori Channappa. Again there is no cross- examination on this document suggesting that the property was not given on rent. Since the documents are executed by the tenant recognizing the terms of the tenancy even in the absence of signatures of the owners and particularly given the fact that the documents are not denied, these two documents can be considered valid piece of evidence. The court is also placing reliance on these documents as unilateral execution of the document is also one of the modes of executing documents where one binds himself to certain conditions by signing the documents. 44. Though one of the documents viz., Ex.P.6 reveals the door number as 1532 and not door number 1535, it is not the case of the respondents that the family- owned one more property bearing No.1532. However, it is 35 to be noticed that the property is said to be located in the same lane where item No.1 property is located. It is not the case of the respondents that one more property was located in the same lane. The promissory notes at Ex.P-8, 9, and 10 would also reveal the address of Kori Veerappa and Kori Channappa as Doddapete in Shimoga. From the reading of these promissory notes executed by Kori Veerappa and Kori Channappa, it is also apparent that both the brothers were jointly carrying on the business by borrowing loans from money lenders and residing together. 45. Though it is urged that Kori Channappa did not take further steps till his lifetime after refusing to give him permission to sue as an indigent person in the year 1936 when he filed a suit for partition against Kori Veerappa the said factor cannot be construed to hold that the claim for partition was abandoned by Kori Veerappa, particularly in a situation where Kori Shanthamma performed the marriage of her sister's daughter Akkamahadevi with appellant no.1 who is none other than the son of Kori Channappa. Though the partition suit was filed in 1935 -36, Kori Shanthamma 36 herself performed the marriage of the 1st plaintiff with Akkamahadevi her sister's daughter. Thereafter, when Akkamahadevi died again Kori Shanthamma performed the marriage of Basavarajappa the plaintiff No.1 in O. S.17 /1977. Thus it is obvious that the relationship was cordial despite the suit for partition was filed in 1935 by Kori Channappa against Kori Veerappa. That seems to be one of the factors that the suit was not prosecuted further as permission to sue as an indigent person was denied. 46. It is also to be taken into consideration that the original title deed pertaining to item No.1 property is produced by plaintiffs in O.S.No.17/1977. Ex.D-3 is the inventory submitted by the Court Commissioner, who was appointed in the suit by Kori Channappa seeking partition. The Court commissioner has noticed that one of the safe lockers found in the suit house i.e., item No.1 property was opened by Kori Channappa. Ex.-D3 is not at all disputed. Both learned counsel appearing for the appellants and respondents would rely upon Ex.D3 to urge a contention that the property exclusively belonged to the respective parties. 37 47. This Court has already noticed that there is a clear contradiction in the stand relating to permissive possession by the defendants in O.S.No.17/1977 and plaintiffs in O.S.No.34/1977 as to how and when the children of Kori Channappa occupied the portion of the suit schedule property at item No.1. This factor if considered in the backdrop of the facts that the original sale deed pertaining to item No.1 property, was produced by the appellants and there being no satisfactory explanation from the respondents as to how the appellants had the custody of the said original title deed, and the how the safe found in the house of Veerappa was opened with the key possessed by his by Channappa. The plea and the evidence relating to permissive possession being very weak would lead to the conclusion that the item No.1 property was jointly possessed by Kori Veerappa and Kori Channappa. 48. The respondents to establish that the appellants did not reside in the item No.1 property till the marriage of 1st appellant with Akkamahadevi would invite the attention of the Court to Ex.D-2 the birth certificate of 38 Nagarajappa-the plaintiff No.1. The said certificate would reveal that he was born on 10.08.1948 and address of his father Kori Channappa is shown as Bikkenahalli, Taluk Shimoga. On consideration of the entire evidence placed on record, it is difficult to accept the contention that Kori Channappa was not a resident of Doddapete, Shimoga where the item No.1 property is located. Quite a few documents referred to above would demonstrate that Kori Channappa was a resident of Doddapete in Shimoga. It is not the case of the respondents that he has a separate house in Doddapete. Hence, too much reliance cannot be placed on Ex.D-2 the birth certificate of plaintiff No.1 to hold that the children of Kori Channappa did not live in Doddapete, Shimoga. 49. The possession of B schedule property by the children of Kori Channapa coupled with the custody of the original title deed pertaining to item No.1 property strongly suggest that the plaintiffs and Kori Shantamma jointly owned it. Though the original title deed pertaining to item no.2 property was also produced by the plaintiffs, the same cannot be construed to hold that item no.2 property 39 is also a joint family property and for this, the court has assigned reasons while dealing with item no.1 property. Thus the production of the original title deed pertaining to item no.1 property by the plaintiffs leads to a totally different conclusion given the fact that plaintiffs are admittedly in possession of B schedule property. 50. By taking into consideration all these factors, this Court is of the view that the Trial Court as well as the First Appellate Court have failed to consider all the material evidence referred to above and also failed to take note of the fact that the original title deed pertaining to item No.1 property came to the custody of the appellants erroneously held that the item No.1 property is the self- acquired property of Kori Veerappa. 51. In the absence of proof relating to the plea of permissive possession alleged by the defendants, the inevitable conclusion is that the item No.1 property is jointly owned and possessed by the appellants and the respondents. 40 52. It is also to be noticed that the plea of the respondents relating to previous partition and adverse possession, pleaded vaguely are not established. The omission to consider this material evidence and appreciate them in a proper perspective has resulted in erroneous judgment. Thus, the 2nd substantial question of law has to be answered in favour of appellants in respect of item No.1 property. Under these circumstances, this Court is of the view that; (a) Item No.1 property was the joint family property of Kori Channappa and Kori Veerappa. (b) Item No.2 property was self-acquired property of Kori Veerappa. (c) The registered Will dated 08.05.1974 executed by Kori Shanthamma is duly proved. (d) The appellants will inherit only ½ share in item no.1 property. (e) The respondents will acquire the entire item no.2 property and ½ share in item no.1 property. 53. For the aforementioned reasons O.S.No.17/ 1977 has to be decreed in part by setting aside the 41 judgment and decree passed by the Trial Court as well as the First Appellate Court and O.S.No.34/1977 has to be decreed in part by setting aside the aforementioned judgment and decree. ORDER (i) RSA No.2708/2006 arising from O.S.No.34/2007 and R.A.No.46/1989 is allowed in part. (ii) RSA No.2707/2006 arising from O.S.No.17/1977 and RA.No.45/1989 is allowed in-part. (iii) Accordingly, judgment and decree dated 31.07.2006 in RA No.46/1989 on the file of Presiding Officer, I Fast Track Court, Shimoga are set aside. (iv) Judgment and decree dated 08.02.1982 in O.S.No.17/1977 on the file of Civil Judge, Shimoga are set aside and the suit O.S.No.17/1977 is decreed in part awarding ½ share in favour of the plaintiffs in respect of item No.1 property and ½ share to the defendants in O.S.No.17/1977 in item No.1 property. And the suit in O.S.No.17/1977 in respect of item No.2 property is dismissed. 42 (v) The judgment and decree dated 31.07.2006 in R.A.No.45/1989 on the file of Presiding Officer, I Fast Track Court, Shimoga are set aside. (vi) Judgment and decree dated 08.02.1982 in O.S.No.34/1977 on the file of Civil Judge, Shimoga are set aside and the suit O.S.No.34/1977 is decreed in part declaring that the plaintiffs are the owners of ½ share in item No.1 property and entire item No.2 property. (vii) The suit in O.S.No.34/1977 is dismissed in respect of prayer for exclusive possession of 'B' schedule property. No order as to costs. Sd/- JUDGE BRN/GVP
